summary:
The path to broad statewide data center moratoriums remained largely closed in late May 2026. Maine's failed veto override on April 29 ended what would have been the first statewide moratorium in US history, and Minnesota's two moratorium bills (HF 4888 and SF 4298) died when the Legislature adjourned on May 18 without a hearing. In their place, narrower ratepayer-protection measures advanced: Oklahoma Governor Stitt signed HB 2992 into law on May 11, requiring large-load customers adding 75 MW or more to cover their own electricity and infrastructure costs, and Washington enacted SB 5982 tying large data centers to utility clean-energy deadlines. New Jersey's large-load utility-tariff bill (S731/A796) is moving through the Senate, while Pennsylvania's data center reporting bill (HB 2150) passed the House but stalled in the Senate.

Virginia, the world's largest data center market, remained deadlocked. Governor Spanberger's May 22 bill-action deadline passed without a state budget, as House and Senate Democrats continued to spar over the roughly $1.6 billion data center sales tax exemption; Spanberger said she would not break a contract the state has signed but signaled openness to not extending the exemption, while Senate Finance Chair Lucas projected a budget by June 30. Ohio's proposed constitutional amendment to ban data centers over 25 MW lagged well behind on signatures — organizers reported roughly 25,000 of the 413,488 required by the July 1 deadline.

Local government is now the most active front, and the pace of new pauses quickened in May. City and county moratoriums took effect in Minneapolis (a six-month pause on facilities over 350,000 sq ft, May 21), Denver (a one-year pause, May 18), Baltimore City (a one-year pause sent to the mayor, May 11), Dubuque County, Iowa, Bloomington and Normal, Illinois, Iron County, Utah, and an 18-month moratorium in Manitowoc County, Wisconsin. Reno became the first Nevada local government to pause new data centers, and Smithfield, Rhode Island banned them outright. Michigan, with more than 20 local moratoriums, added Meridian and Macomb County's Washington townships. Not every effort succeeded: the Cheyenne, Wyoming council rejected a moratorium 8-1, a Pulaski County, Arkansas measure fell one vote short, and a developer sued to overturn Hill County, Texas's new pause.

Construction continued to outpace policy. Anthropic agreed on May 6 to use the full compute of xAI's Colossus 1 in Memphis (about 220,000-plus GPUs), even as the NAACP sought an emergency injunction over the unpermitted gas turbines powering the adjacent Colossus 2. Meta's $27 billion Hyperion campus in Louisiana reached roughly 4,000 construction workers and won about $3.3 billion in state tax breaks in mid-May. OpenAI's Stargate program expanded again, with SoftBank's SB Energy named on May 9 to build and operate a 1.2 GW site in Milam County, Texas, alongside ongoing builds in Abilene and Shackelford County. New mega-campuses advanced elsewhere, including an approved project near Joliet, Illinois of up to 1.8 GW and Aligned's 540 MW Project Caprock near Abernathy, Texas. The overall trajectory points toward incremental ratepayer regulation, locally driven pauses, and mounting environmental and utility-cost litigation rather than statewide bans — while frontier AI construction accelerates.
